content.text: is a React wrapper component that enables additional development-only checks. Its most visible behavior is
certain functions to detect side effects that would break concurrent rendering.

When StrictMode is active, React deliberately calls your component function twice per render cycle — discarding the first result and committing the second. This makes impure components reveal themselves: if the component has a side effect (mutates external state, produces different output on repeated calls), the two invocations will produce inconsistent results that are immediately visible.
. Production builds call each component exactly once. The double-invoke is a development CI check, not a production behavior change.

attrs.alt: Diagram comparing single-invocation normal mode vs StrictMode double-invocation and how it exposes impure renders

You can also wrap individual subtrees rather than the entire app. This is useful during migrations — you can progressively opt subtrees into StrictMode checks:
StrictMode double-invokes the following functions in development:
Detect side effects in initial state
cleanup+setup happens once on mount: React runs setup, immediately runs cleanup, then runs setup again. This mimics what happens in concurrent mode when React unmounts and remounts components.
. This is disorienting but intentional — it is telling you that your render is impure. The fix is to move the counter into state or derive it without mutation.
StrictMode also double-runs effects on mount. The sequence is:
Mount: React runs the component and commits the DOM.
Setup: useEffect setup function runs (as normal).
Cleanup: React immediately runs the cleanup function as if the component unmounted.
Setup again: React runs the setup function again — simulating a remount.
This sequence detects effects that do not clean up properly — a common source of bugs in concurrent mode, where React may unmount and remount components during transitions.
The correct mental model: your effect
. If the effect is run twice, the world should end up in the same state as if it were run once. This is achievable by making cleanup fully reverse setup:
(passing a function) is also double-invoked in StrictMode:
Pure initializers that only compute a value are fine — they produce the same result both times. Impure ones that mutate external state reveal themselves through the inconsistency.
reducers are also double-invoked in StrictMode for each dispatch. This catches reducers that mutate state directly:
A common StrictMode experience is seeing every
in a component body printed twice in the browser console. This is expected — it confirms StrictMode is active and double-invoking your component:

To distinguish StrictMode double-logs from real double renders: React uses a slightly dimmed style for the discarded invocation's logs in the Chrome console. The second (real) invocation appears at full brightness.
If double-logging is distracting, you can install the
browser extension — it styles the discarded invocations differently, making it easier to read.
StrictMode vs Production: The Key Difference
. Every double-invocation, every extra effect cycle, every extra log is absent in production:

This means if a bug only shows up in development under StrictMode, it is almost certainly an impurity that would also appear in production concurrent mode — just less predictably. StrictMode surfaces it reliably so you can fix it before production.
My effect is running twice on mount

Expected behavior in StrictMode. This simulates what happens in concurrent mode when React remounts components during a transition or hydration. Make your effect resilient to setup+cleanup+setup by writing a proper cleanup function.

In development under StrictMode, one-shot side effects fire twice. This is expected. Production will fire once. If you need exact counts in development too, use the ref guard pattern shown earlier. Most analytics tools are fine with the occasional dev double-fire.
My component shows wrong initial state

If your state initializer mutates external state, StrictMode reveals it by calling the initializer twice. The second call produces a different initial value. Fix: make the initializer pure — compute and return a value without mutating anything outside the function.
Removing StrictMode makes the bug disappear

This means the bug depends on double-invocation to manifest. The underlying impurity still exists and will appear in production concurrent mode when React pauses and restarts renders. Fix the impurity rather than removing StrictMode.
StrictMode does more than catch impure renders. In React 18+, it also:
findDOMNode, legacy Context API, string refs, and other deprecated patterns trigger warnings only in StrictMode.
Missing keys in lists generate warnings that help you find performance issues before they become hard-to-debug reconciliation bugs.
The effect remount cycle mimics the component lifecycle in concurrent mode, helping you write effects that work correctly in both rendering modes.

StrictMode's double-rendering is a development-only safety net that surfaces impurity bugs before they reach production. By calling component functions, state initializers, reducers, and effect setups twice, React verifies that each is idempotent — that repeating the call produces the same observable result. Components that mutate external state, initialize state impurely, or write side effects in the render body will produce inconsistent results that are immediately visible. Effects that do not clean up properly are exposed by the setup+cleanup+setup cycle. None of this affects production — it is exclusively a development quality tool.
StrictMode's double-invoke extends to memoized values and context providers:

Context providers are not double-invoked themselves, but their consumers are (as component functions). If your context value is derived from a computation inside a component body, that computation runs twice per render under StrictMode.
Some third-party libraries were authored before React's concurrent model and have impure render or effect patterns. Common symptoms when running StrictMode with older libraries:
Libraries that subscribe to stores inside effects may create two subscriptions if they don't implement cleanup. StrictMode exposes this immediately.
Legacy analytics hooks that call track() in a useEffect without cleanup fire twice. This is expected in dev but correct in production.
Old component libraries may use deprecated APIs (findDOMNode, legacy refs) that StrictMode warns about.
When you encounter StrictMode warnings from a library, check if a newer version has resolved them. Many popular libraries updated their React compatibility as part of their React 18 migration.
If you're adding StrictMode to a large existing codebase, you may find many warnings at once. A gradual approach:
Wrap only new features in StrictMode initially. Let existing code stay outside.
Fix StrictMode violations as they are found during normal development — don't try to fix everything at once.
Expand the StrictMode wrapper as each section is cleaned up.
Aim for the entire app to eventually be wrapped, as that is what create-react-app and Vite React templates do by default.
React Testing Library wraps renders in
but does not automatically enable StrictMode. To test your components under the same conditions as your app, wrap your test renderer with StrictMode:
Testing with StrictMode reveals the same class of bugs during test runs that StrictMode reveals in the browser — making your test suite a stronger guard against impurity regressions.

StrictMode double-invokes component body, state initializers, reducers, and effects in development only. If your component produces different output on the two calls, it is impure. Effect double-invocation (setup+cleanup+setup) requires a proper cleanup function to pass. Removing StrictMode to make a bug disappear means the impurity still exists and will surface in production concurrent mode.
